text: The surgeon lifts the middle lobe to expose the pulmonary artery within the oblique fissure. The lower lobe is positioned at the bottom of the field. Careful dissection is performed due to visible lymph nodes and anatomical variations. The bronchoscope light is visible inside the trachea in the background.

The surgeon divides the posterior oblique fissure, revealing the right main bronchus which branches into the upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius. An unusual vein from the posterior segment of the upper lobe is dissected, extending to the apex of the lower lobe. The horizontal fissure is divided, and R-eleven lymph nodes are removed beneath the aberrant pulmonary vein. The dissection continues on the pulmonary artery, identifying middle lobe branches and another R-eleven lymph node, while lifting the aberrant vein to expose a posterior ascending arterial branch.

The surgeon dissects the inferior pulmonary ligament and removes R9 lymph nodes, followed by dissection of the pleura posterior to the inferior pulmonary ligament along the bronchus intermedius. The dissection continues between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, encountering an unusual vein. A small branch of this vein is cauterized, and further dissection of R11 lymph nodes reveals arterial and venous anatomy. The procedure progresses to lymphadenectomy in the 2R and 4R regions, with the pleura opened posterior to the superior vena cava and over the azygous vein.

The surgeon removes a large 4R lymph node packet using a glove finger technique. Returning to the fissure, the aberrant vein from the posterior segment of the right upper lobe vein to the apex of the lower lobe is visualized and stapled. Behind this, the posterior ascending branch to the upper lobe is preserved. The posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery is now clearly visible. Further dissection of R-XI and R-XII lymph nodes next to the upper lobe bronchus is performed, considering the N-I positivity observed in the scan.

The surgeon uses a robotic arm to dissect and staple the middle lobe vein, followed by the division of another middle lobe vein. The lower lobe vein is also divided using a stapler. Returning to the fissure, the pulmonary artery is visible, and the absence of the middle lobe vein allows for the safe completion of the horizontal fissure with a stapler.

The surgeon divides the lower and middle lobe branches of the pulmonary artery using two staple fires, ensuring preservation of the posterior ascending branch to the upper lobe. The bronchus intermedius is stapled below the right upper lobe bronchus under bronchoscopic vision. The lower and middle lobes are removed en bloc as a single specimen, placed in a plastic bag, and extracted through a low anterior assistant port.
The surgeon performs a meticulous check of the surgical field, ensuring all lymph nodes are removed, guided by PET scan findings.
